meta.disclaimer: AI-generated; mirrors MCAT CARS format but is NOT real MCAT/AAMC content and may not match real difficulty/formatting.

passageSets.title: The Rule and the Result
passageSets.passage:
Few disputes in moral philosophy prove as durable as the quarrel between those who judge actions by the rules they obey and those who judge them by the outcomes they produce. The first camp, often called deontologists, insists that certain acts are forbidden or required regardless of their results; a promise binds, a lie corrupts, and no favorable tally of consequences can launder a betrayal. The second camp, the consequentialists, finds such rigidity absurd. For them, morality is a matter of arithmetic performed upon human welfare: an act is right precisely insofar as it produces more good than any available alternative, and a rule deserves respect only when obeying it does more good than breaking it.

The consequentialist position enjoys an obvious appeal. It promises to settle disputes by measurement rather than by appeal to intuition or ancient authority, and it refuses to let anyone hide cruelty behind the letter of a rule. If following a principle predictably ruins lives, the consequentialist asks, why should we prize the principle over the lives it was meant to protect? Put this way, the doctrine sounds less like a theory than like plain sensibility, and its defenders present it as the natural creed of anyone who genuinely cares about suffering rather than about keeping their own hands ceremonially clean. The appeal, in short, is the appeal of honesty about what genuinely matters.

Yet the arithmetic conceals a difficulty that its confidence tends to obscure. To act well, the consequentialist must forecast outcomes, and human beings forecast badly, especially when their own interests quietly tilt the scales. A rule against torture, or against breaking a solemn promise, is valuable precisely because it does not bend to the clever calculations of the moment, calculations that reliably flatter whoever happens to be performing them. History offers grim examples of atrocities excused, in advance, by confident predictions of the good they would supposedly secure. Rules, on this view, are not obstacles to good judgment but hard-won substitutes for a judgment we have learned not to trust; they encode the accumulated experience that certain acts, however tempting their projected payoff, corrode both the agent who commits them and the community that quietly permits them.

The wiser conclusion, I think, is that the two camps need each other more than either likes to admit. Rules without any regard for consequences harden into cruelty dressed as virtue, while consequences without any regard for rules dissolve into a permission to do anything, provided one tells oneself a sufficiently hopeful story. A mature morality treats rules as the settled verdicts of past reasoning about consequences, to be overridden only when the case for doing so is overwhelming rather than merely convenient. That standard will not silence the quarrel, but it at least locates the burden of proof where it belongs: on the one who would break faith. Neither camp, taken alone, can be trusted with a hard case; together they supply what each separately lacks.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward pure consequentialism is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: enthusiastic endorsement of its method

choices.label: B
choices.text: hostile dismissal of its motives

choices.label: C
choices.text: qualified skepticism that grants its appeal

choices.label: D
choices.text: complete indifference to the debate

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author credits consequentialism with an 'obvious appeal' yet argues its arithmetic 'conceals a difficulty,' which is exactly qualified skepticism. A is wrong because the author never endorses the method and instead warns against resting morality on it. B is wrong because a hostile dismissal would not concede the view's genuine appeal, as the author explicitly does. D is wrong because the author is plainly engaged rather than indifferent, devoting the essay to weighing the position.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The primary function of the second paragraph (beginning 'The consequentialist position') is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: present the strongest case for a view the author will later qualify

choices.label: B
choices.text: refute consequentialism by means of counterexamples

choices.label: C
choices.text: define deontology in technical terms

choices.label: D
choices.text: propose the author's own compromise position

questions.explanation: A is correct: the paragraph sympathetically lays out the appeal of consequentialism before the author complicates it in the following paragraph. B is wrong because the refutation, the point about bad forecasting, appears in the third paragraph, not here. C is wrong because deontology is defined in the first paragraph, not the second. D is wrong because the author's compromise is reserved for the final paragraph.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: It can be inferred that the author regards moral rules primarily as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: divine commands that require no justification

choices.label: B
choices.text: arbitrary conventions that vary freely by culture

choices.label: C
choices.text: obstacles that mature agents learn to ignore

choices.label: D
choices.text: distilled conclusions of earlier reasoning about consequences

questions.explanation: D is correct: the author calls rules 'the settled verdicts of past reasoning about consequences,' making them distilled conclusions. A is wrong because the author never grounds rules in divine command; their authority comes from accumulated experience. B is wrong because the author treats rules as 'hard-won,' not arbitrary. C is wrong because it reverses the author's view: rules are substitutes for untrustworthy judgment and should be overridden only rarely, not ignored.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which scenario best exemplifies the danger the author attributes to unchecked consequentialist reasoning?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A judge refuses a bribe despite personal hardship

choices.label: B
choices.text: An official breaks a promise, justifying it by predicting benefits that happen to serve his own ambition

choices.label: C
choices.text: A citizen obeys a law she personally finds inconvenient

choices.label: D
choices.text: A scholar measures two theories against shared intuitions

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author warns that outcome forecasts 'reliably flatter whoever' makes them, which is precisely the self-serving prediction in B. A is wrong because refusing a bribe is rule-following, not the danger described. C is wrong because obeying an inconvenient law again illustrates rule-following rather than consequentialist overreach. D is wrong because comparing theories against intuitions is neutral methodology, not the abuse the author fears.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's defense of rules?

choices.label: A
choices.text: People predict the consequences of breaking promises far more accurately than the author assumes

choices.label: B
choices.text: Powerful individuals have often broken promises for selfish reasons

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most moral rules were originally adopted to prevent specific harms

choices.label: D
choices.text: People tend to rationalize their misconduct after committing it

questions.explanation: A is correct: the defense of rules rests on the premise that humans forecast outcomes badly; showing they forecast well removes that support. B is wrong because selfish promise-breaking supports, rather than weakens, the author's worry about self-interest. C is wrong because rules adopted to prevent harm fits the author's picture of rules as distilled consequences. D is wrong because a tendency to rationalize reinforces the author's distrust of case-by-case judgment.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's argument depends on which assumption?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consequences are impossible to estimate in any situation

choices.label: B
choices.text: Deontological rules never conflict with one another

choices.label: C
choices.text: People's predictions of outcomes are systematically biased by self-interest

choices.label: D
choices.text: Moral disputes can be resolved by measurement alone

questions.explanation: C is correct: the case for rules assumes that self-interest predictably distorts our forecasts, which is why rules that resist momentary calculation are valuable. A is wrong because the author says we forecast 'badly,' not that estimation is impossible. B is wrong because the author never claims rules avoid conflict; indeed rigid rules can 'harden into cruelty.' D is wrong because resolution by measurement is the consequentialist claim the author actively doubts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: In Praise of Doubt
passageSets.passage:
We are taught to prize certainty and to treat doubt as a symptom of weakness, as though the confident mind were the healthy one and the hesitant mind merely unfinished. This inherited prejudice deserves examination. The history of inquiry suggests almost the reverse: that certainty is where thought goes to die, and that doubt, far from being the enemy of knowledge, is the restless faculty that produces it. Every correction we have ever made to our picture of the world began as a suspicion that what everyone knew might not be so. To honor certainty above all may be to honor the very habit that keeps our errors comfortably in place.

The craving for certainty is understandable, since uncertainty is uncomfortable and decisions must be made. But the craving is dangerous when it hardens into dogmatism, the state of holding a belief so tightly that no evidence could loosen it. The dogmatist mistakes the intensity of a conviction for a measure of its truth, and confuses the comfort a belief provides with the reasons that might support it. Such a mind is not strong but brittle, for it has forfeited the capacity to learn, and a belief that cannot be revised is indistinguishable, from the inside, from a belief that merely happens to be comforting.

Productive doubt is something else entirely. It is not the lazy shrug that declares every opinion as good as any other, but a disciplined refusal to accept more than the evidence warrants. The careful inquirer treats each conclusion as provisional, holds it exactly as firmly as the reasons allow, and remains alert to the discovery that would require revision. This posture is demanding rather than comfortable, and it explains why genuine skepticism has always been rarer, and more strenuous, than the confident assertion it is so often mistaken for. It asks us to tolerate the discomfort of an open question rather than to purchase relief with a premature answer.

Still, doubt has its own excesses, and honesty requires admitting them. A doubt that questions everything at once, including the standards by which questioning proceeds, saws off the branch it sits upon and collapses into paralysis or pose. The point of doubting is eventually to believe something better, not to luxuriate in suspension forever. The doubt worth defending is therefore instrumental: a tool for improving beliefs, aimed at truth and disciplined by evidence. Understood that way, doubt is not the opposite of conviction but its most reliable source, the only route by which a conviction earns the right to be held at all. To defend doubt, in the end, is simply to insist that a belief earn its keep before it is believed.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The central claim of the passage is that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: certainty and doubt are equally valuable in every circumstance

choices.label: B
choices.text: disciplined doubt, rather than certainty, is the true engine of knowledge

choices.label: C
choices.text: all beliefs should be suspended indefinitely

choices.label: D
choices.text: dogmatism is preferable to paralysis

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author argues that 'certainty is where thought goes to die' and that productive doubt is what produces knowledge. A is wrong because the author ranks disciplined doubt above certainty rather than treating them as equals. C is wrong because the final paragraph condemns endless suspension as a collapse into paralysis. D is wrong because the author criticizes both dogmatism and paralysis, preferring neither.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author discusses dogmatism primarily in order to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: praise the strength of confident minds

choices.label: B
choices.text: show that certainty is always impossible

choices.label: C
choices.text: define productive doubt through an example

choices.label: D
choices.text: illustrate the cost of craving certainty

questions.explanation: D is correct: dogmatism is presented as what happens when the craving for certainty 'hardens,' illustrating that craving's cost. A is wrong because the author calls the dogmatic mind 'brittle,' not strong. B is wrong because the author does not claim certainty is universally impossible, only that clinging to it is dangerous. C is wrong because productive doubt is defined by contrast in a later paragraph, not exemplified by dogmatism.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage implies that a belief held so firmly that no evidence could revise it is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: indistinguishable, from the inside, from a merely comforting belief

choices.label: B
choices.text: more likely to be true than a provisional belief

choices.label: C
choices.text: the mark of a disciplined inquirer

choices.label: D
choices.text: the natural product of productive doubt

questions.explanation: A is correct: the author states that such a belief is, from the believer's perspective, no different from one that merely feels good. B is wrong because the author denies that intensity of conviction tracks truth. C is wrong because the disciplined inquirer holds beliefs provisionally, the opposite of unrevisable. D is wrong because unrevisable belief is dogmatism, which productive doubt is meant to prevent.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A scientist who abandons a favored hypothesis after a well-designed study contradicts it best embodies:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the dogmatism the author criticizes

choices.label: B
choices.text: the corrosive doubt the author warns against

choices.label: C
choices.text: the productive doubt the author endorses

choices.label: D
choices.text: the craving for certainty the author diagnoses

questions.explanation: C is correct: revising a belief in light of evidence is exactly the disciplined, evidence-governed doubt the author praises. A is wrong because a dogmatist would refuse to revise. B is wrong because corrosive doubt questions everything indiscriminately and yields paralysis, not a targeted revision. D is wrong because the scientist relinquishes certainty rather than craving it.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which claim, if true, would most strengthen the author's distinction between productive and corrosive doubt?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most people find uncertainty pleasant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Skepticism and dogmatism reliably produce identical results

choices.label: C
choices.text: All doubt eventually leads to paralysis

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inquirers who hold conclusions provisionally correct their errors more often than those who doubt indiscriminately

questions.explanation: D is correct: showing that provisional (productive) doubt outperforms indiscriminate (corrosive) doubt directly supports the author's distinction between them. A is wrong because it contradicts the author's premise that uncertainty is uncomfortable and says nothing about the distinction. B is wrong because equating skepticism with dogmatism would collapse a different distinction, not reinforce the productive-corrosive one. C is wrong because it would erase the category of productive doubt entirely.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The argument assumes that the value of doubt lies in its:

choices.label: A
choices.text: capacity to make every opinion equally good

choices.label: B
choices.text: usefulness as a means to better beliefs

choices.label: C
choices.text: power to provide emotional comfort

choices.label: D
choices.text: ability to bring inquiry to an end

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author frames worthwhile doubt as 'instrumental,' a tool aimed at improving beliefs and reaching truth. A is wrong because the author explicitly rejects the shrug that treats all opinions as equal. C is wrong because comfort is what certainty offers, not the point of doubt. D is wrong because doubt sustains inquiry rather than ending it.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: Where Beauty Lives
passageSets.passage:
Ask whether beauty lives in the artwork or in the eye that beholds it, and you revive one of the oldest quarrels in aesthetics, one that refuses to be settled because each side has hold of a genuine truth and mistakes it for the whole. To say beauty is simply out there, a property of the canvas as its dimensions are, cannot be right; yet to say it is merely in here, a private flutter of the nerves, seems equally false to the experience of standing before something and feeling that it demands admiration.

The objectivist tradition locates beauty in features of the thing: proportion, symmetry, the harmonious ordering of parts into a whole. There is something to this. We do point to qualities in the work when we defend our judgments, and we do not regard all such defenses as equally good. But the objectivist overreaches. If beauty were a property like mass, disagreement about it would be simple error, and the trained eye of one age would not so regularly find hideous what an earlier age found sublime. Standards migrate; the canvas does not. A property that shifts with the observer is a strange sort of property indeed.

The subjectivist seizes on exactly this variability. Beauty, on this view, names nothing in the object and everything in the response; to call a thing beautiful is merely to report that one happens to like it. This dissolves the puzzle by dissolving the subject matter, and it exacts too high a price. It cannot explain why we argue about art at all, why we take some judgments to be more cultivated than others, or why a lifetime of looking can teach a person to see what she once walked past. Mere preference neither improves nor errs.

The more defensible position treats beauty as relational: a disposition of certain objects to produce a distinctive response in perceivers who have been suitably prepared to receive it. On this account the qualities the objectivist prizes are real, but they are beautiful only in relation to a sensibility that education, culture, and attention have shaped. Taste is neither a fixed measurement nor an arbitrary whim but an achievement, something one can do well or badly, refine or neglect. That is why beauty can be both taught and disputed, and why the quarrel endures: each camp guards half of a truth that only makes sense whole. It also explains our willingness to trust a cultivated judge over a careless one, a deference that would be senseless if beauty were either a plain fact or a mere feeling. Beauty, then, is neither wholly found nor wholly made, but met, in the encounter of a made thing with a mind made ready for it.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage is primarily concerned with:

choices.label: A
choices.text: proving that beauty is an objective property of artworks

choices.label: B
choices.text: showing that all judgments of beauty are equally valid

choices.label: C
choices.text: tracing the reception history of a single artwork

choices.label: D
choices.text: defending a relational account of beauty against purely objective and purely subjective ones

questions.explanation: D is correct: the author sets up both traditions and then argues for a relational view that treats beauty as a disposition to affect prepared perceivers. A is wrong because the author explicitly rejects pure objectivism. B is wrong because the author denies that all judgments are equally good, insisting some are more cultivated. C is wrong because no single artwork's history is traced.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward both the objectivist and subjectivist traditions is best characterized as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: wholesale rejection of each

choices.label: B
choices.text: appreciative but critical, crediting each with a partial truth

choices.label: C
choices.text: neutral reportage without evaluation

choices.label: D
choices.text: enthusiastic endorsement of the subjectivist

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author says each side 'has hold of a genuine truth' yet 'overreaches,' which is appreciation tempered by criticism. A is wrong because the author preserves the valid core of each rather than rejecting them outright. C is wrong because the author clearly evaluates, judging each position's limits. D is wrong because subjectivism is faulted for exacting 'too high a price.'

questions.stem: The remark that 'standards migrate; the canvas does not' is meant to imply that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: artworks physically change over time

choices.label: B
choices.text: beauty is a measurable property like mass

choices.label: C
choices.text: shifting judgments across eras count against locating beauty solely in the object

choices.label: D
choices.text: no one can ever agree about art

questions.explanation: C is correct: because the object stays fixed while judgments change, the variation must lie in perceivers, undercutting pure objectivism. A is wrong because the line says the canvas does NOT change, not that it does. B is wrong because the remark argues against, not for, treating beauty as a mass-like property. D is wrong because the author elsewhere affirms that cultivated agreement is possible.

questions.stem: Which situation best fits the author's relational view of beauty?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A novice, after years of study, comes to admire a work she once found dull

choices.label: B
choices.text: A critic measures a sculpture and pronounces it beautiful by its dimensions

choices.label: C
choices.text: A viewer insists that his liking a poster settles that it is beautiful

choices.label: D
choices.text: An appraiser weighs a painting to determine its market value

questions.explanation: A is correct: the relational view holds that taste is an achievement, so a cultivated sensibility can newly perceive beauty, exactly what the novice's growth shows. B is wrong because judging by dimensions alone is pure objectivism. C is wrong because reducing beauty to personal liking is pure subjectivism. D is wrong because market weighing is irrelevant to aesthetic response.

questions.stem: Which finding would most WEAKEN the author's relational account?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Training in an art form reliably changes what people find beautiful

choices.label: B
choices.text: Judgments of beauty are identical across all cultures and all levels of training, regardless of preparation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Experts and novices often disagree about the same work

choices.label: D
choices.text: People defend aesthetic judgments by pointing to features of the work

questions.explanation: B is correct: the relational account predicts that preparation shapes response, so uniform judgments independent of preparation would undermine it. A is wrong because training-driven change supports the account. C is wrong because expert-novice disagreement is consistent with differing preparation. D is wrong because the author grants that object features matter, so this fits the view.

questions.stem: The author's relational account assumes that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: beauty can exist with no perceiver at all

choices.label: B
choices.text: all perceivers respond to a work identically

choices.label: C
choices.text: aesthetic preferences cannot be educated

choices.label: D
choices.text: some perceivers are better prepared than others to respond to a work

questions.explanation: D is correct: the account depends on 'suitably prepared' perceivers whose taste has been cultivated, implying differences in preparation. A is wrong because a relational view requires a perceiver for beauty to arise. B is wrong because the author stresses that responses vary with preparation. C is wrong because the author says taste can be refined through education and attention.

passageSets.title: The Uses of Metaphor
passageSets.passage:
It is tempting to regard metaphor as a kind of verbal ornament, a splash of color that poets add to a meaning that could, if one insisted, be stated plainly. On this familiar view the literal comes first and the figurative second; metaphor is a detour, pleasant but dispensable, around the straight road of literal truth. I want to suggest that this picture inverts the actual relationship, and that metaphor is less a decoration upon thought than one of the structures that make abstract thought possible in the first place. The stakes are not merely literary, for how we picture metaphor shapes how we picture the mind.

Consider how much of our supposedly plain speech is metaphor grown so familiar we no longer notice it. We grasp an idea, follow an argument, build a case, and defend a position; we speak of warm welcomes and cold receptions, of high hopes and low spirits, of time that is spent, saved, wasted, and running out. None of these expressions is poetic in any ornamental sense, yet each imports the shape of one domain, often something bodily or spatial, onto another that has no shape of its own. Strip the metaphors away and it is not clear that anything articulate remains.

If that is right, then metaphors do real cognitive work: they lend borrowed structure to matters we could not otherwise handle. But structure always comes at a price, for every metaphor highlights some features of its subject while hiding others. To treat time as money makes its scarcity vivid and its other qualities invisible; to cast an argument as war foregrounds winning and obscures the possibility that both parties might learn. The metaphor we reach for silently settles which questions seem natural and which never arise at all. What we can imagine about a subject is quietly governed by the figure through which we approach it.

The honest conclusion is double-edged. We cannot escape metaphor into some pure literal language, because the abstract vocabulary we would escape into is itself sedimented metaphor. But we are not therefore its prisoners. To notice the metaphor we are using, to ask what it hides as well as what it reveals, and to try another in its place is among the most powerful moves available to a careful thinker. Metaphor cannot be abolished; it can, however, be handled with our eyes open, and that difference is the whole of the matter. The careful thinker does not flee metaphor but chooses it deliberately, aware that every choice both opens and forecloses.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage argues chiefly that metaphor is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: a structure that makes abstract thought possible, not merely decorative

choices.label: B
choices.text: an obstacle that clear thinkers should eliminate

choices.label: C
choices.text: a device useful only in poetry

choices.label: D
choices.text: identical in effect to literal language

questions.explanation: A is correct: the author claims metaphor is 'one of the structures that make abstract thought possible,' inverting the ornament view. B is wrong because the author says metaphor is inescapable and cognitively valuable, not to be eliminated. C is wrong because the essay stresses everyday, non-poetic metaphor. D is wrong because metaphor selectively highlights and hides, unlike neutral literal speech.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's stance toward the view that metaphor is mere ornament is:

choices.text: accepting

choices.label: B
choices.text: indifferent

choices.label: C
choices.text: corrective

choices.label: D
choices.text: mocking

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author explicitly aims to show that the ornament view 'inverts the actual relationship,' seeking to correct it. A is wrong because the author rejects that view. B is wrong because the author engages it at length rather than ignoring it. D is wrong because the tone is analytical rather than mocking or derisive.

questions.stem: The list of everyday expressions in the second paragraph functions to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: prove that metaphor belongs only to poets

choices.label: B
choices.text: show that ordinary language is saturated with unnoticed metaphor

choices.label: C
choices.text: define the term 'literal'

choices.label: D
choices.text: argue that metaphors never mislead

A is wrong because the examples are ordinary, not poetic, undercutting the poets-only claim. C is wrong because no definition of 'literal' is offered there. D is wrong because the next paragraph insists metaphors do hide features, so it does not argue they never mislead.

questions.stem: According to the passage, a thinker who says 'let us stop treating this negotiation as a battle and instead as a shared problem' is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: escaping metaphor into literal language

choices.label: B
choices.text: proving that metaphor is merely decorative

choices.label: C
choices.text: abolishing metaphor altogether

choices.label: D
choices.text: exercising the careful move the author recommends by swapping one metaphor for another

questions.explanation: D is correct: the author praises noticing a metaphor and trying another in its place, which is exactly what swapping 'battle' for 'shared problem' does. A is wrong because both phrases are metaphors, so no literal escape occurs. B is wrong because the swap shows metaphor's power to shape thought, not its decorativeness. C is wrong because the author says metaphor 'cannot be abolished.'

questions.stem: Which observation, if true, would most strengthen the author's claim that metaphors hide as well as highlight?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Poets use more metaphors than other writers

choices.label: B
choices.text: People enjoy vivid figurative language

choices.label: C
choices.text: Groups that describe a dispute as war overlook cooperative solutions that groups using other metaphors readily propose

choices.label: D
choices.text: Some metaphors are more common than others

questions.explanation: C is correct: it shows a metaphor foregrounding some options while concealing others, directly supporting the highlight-and-hide claim. A is wrong because frequency of use by poets says nothing about selective concealment. B is wrong because enjoyment is unrelated to what a metaphor hides. D is wrong because relative commonness does not bear on the highlighting-and-hiding function.

questions.stem: The argument rests on the assumption that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: our abstract vocabulary is itself built from metaphor

choices.label: B
choices.text: literal language is impossible to understand

choices.label: C
choices.text: metaphors are always misleading

choices.label: D
choices.text: poetry is superior to prose

questions.explanation: A is correct: the author's claim that we cannot escape into 'some pure literal language' assumes that even abstract terms are 'sedimented metaphor.' B is wrong because the author never says literal language is unintelligible, only that it is not metaphor-free. C is wrong because the author holds that metaphors can be handled well, not that they always mislead. D is wrong because no ranking of poetry over prose is assumed.

passageSets.title: The Voice We Cannot Trust
passageSets.passage:
Every story reaches us through a voice, and we are inclined, out of habit and courtesy, to trust that voice. The unreliable narrator is the device that turns this trust into a problem. Such a narrator tells the tale, but the telling cannot be taken at face value: through self-deception, limited knowledge, or open design, the account we are given diverges from the account the work quietly invites us to construct. Reading well then requires a double attention, one ear on what is said and another on what the saying betrays.

The technique works by opening a gap. A narrator insists upon his own generosity while every remembered scene reveals his meanness; a governess reports supernatural terrors that may be the shadows of her own unsettled mind. The author supplies, alongside the narrator's words, small contradictions and telling details that the narrator does not weigh but the reader cannot ignore. Meaning collects in that space between statement and evidence, and the reader must do the collecting, since no reliable voice steps forward to do it for her. The pleasure and the labor of such reading are one and the same.

This is precisely the source of the device's power. An unreliable narrator converts reading from passive reception into active investigation. We are no longer told what to think about the characters; we are made to judge them, and to notice ourselves judging. Trust itself becomes the subject: how readily we extend it, what evidence should qualify it, and how uncomfortable it is to withdraw it from a voice that has grown familiar and even dear. We become, in effect, jurors weighing a witness whose interest in the verdict we cannot forget.

There is a risk, of course, that the whole performance will curdle into a mere trick, a puzzle whose only reward is the flattery of having solved it. Handled cynically, unreliability cheapens into a twist. But handled well, it does something no trustworthy narration can, precisely because we have felt, from the inside, how sympathy and suspicion contend when the evidence is partial and the witness is interested. The unreliable narrator is therefore not a betrayal of the reader so much as an education in how carefully that trust ought to be given. What looks like a betrayal of the reader's trust is, at its best, a lesson in how carefully that trust should be spent.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage's main point is that the unreliable narrator:

choices.label: A
choices.text: should be avoided because it deceives readers

choices.label: B
choices.text: is merely a fashionable trick with no real value

choices.label: C
choices.text: engages readers as active interpreters and makes the giving of trust its subject

choices.label: D
choices.text: always signals its own unreliability openly

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author argues the device turns reading into 'active investigation' and makes trust itself the theme. A is wrong because the author defends, rather than warns against, the technique. B is wrong because the author says at its best it achieves something no reliable narration can. D is wrong because unreliability can arise from self-deception or limited knowledge, not always open design.

B is wrong because the author values the technique highly. C is wrong because nothing in the tone suggests alarm. D is wrong because the author is clearly engaged and favorable, not indifferent.

A is wrong because the examples do not claim every narrator is unreliable. B is wrong because no genre is ranked as superior. C is wrong because 'author' is not being defined by these instances.

passageSets.title: Faithful by Re-Creation
passageSets.passage:
The comforting ideal of translation is that of a transparent pane of glass: the translator should disappear, leaving the reader with the author's meaning undistorted, as though no second mind had intervened. On this ideal the only virtue is fidelity, and the only fault is the intrusion of the translator's own voice. It is a tidy picture, and almost everything about the actual practice of translation conspires to show that it is false.

Consider what fidelity would require. Words carry not only definitions but music, history, and connotation, and no two languages distribute these alike. A term that is plain in one tongue arrives grand or comic in another; a pun that turns a scene cannot survive a literal rendering; the rhythm that gives a line its force has no equivalent in a language built on different sounds. The translator who clings to the letter betrays the spirit, producing something accurate in its parts and dead as a whole. Fidelity to the surface can be the surest route to infidelity of sense.

What the translator actually does, then, is closer to re-creation than to copying. Faced with effects that cannot be transferred, she must decide which to sacrifice and which to reproduce by other means, inventing in her own language a new thing that does what the original did. This makes her something like a second author, and it is why we can speak of a great translation as a work in its own right, and why the same poem in two translators' hands can become two genuinely different poems. That is why the translator's name, once an embarrassment, increasingly shares the title page.

None of this licenses mere invention, and here the defenders of fidelity recover their point. Re-creation is disciplined by a debt to the original; the translator is free, but freely bound, answerable at every turn to an effect she did not choose. The truest fidelity, then, is not to the words but to what the words accomplish, and achieving it demands exactly the creative liberty the transparent-pane ideal forbids. Translation is neither servile copying nor free composition but the difficult art of being faithful by re-creating, a paradox the practice cannot escape and should stop apologizing for. The best translators have always known this, laboring not to vanish but to make something live again in a new tongue.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: The Thread of the Self
passageSets.passage:
What makes the child in an old photograph the same person as the adult who now holds it? Almost nothing physical has survived the interval; the cells have turned over, the face is unrecognizable, the tastes and opinions have reversed. If the body is a river that never contains the same water twice, then bodily continuity seems too thin a thread to carry the weight of identity. Something else must bind the years together, and the most natural candidate is memory. The question is not idle, for nearly everything we care about presupposes that persons persist.

The memory theory proposes that you are the same person as some earlier being if you can remember, from the inside, that being's experiences. The thread of the self is a chain of recollection: I am continuous with the child in the photograph because I remember, however dimly, being her, and she is continuous with an infant she could once recall. Identity, on this account, is not a substance that persists but a relation that links successive experiences into a single point of view.

The theory is elegant and immediately troubled. I have forgotten almost everything about most days of my life; on the strict memory view those forgotten stretches threaten to fall outside the self entirely. Worse, memory is not a faithful record but a reconstruction, capable of confident error, so that a vivid false memory would make me continuous with a person who never existed. And there is a circularity lurking nearby, for to say that a memory is genuinely mine already seems to presuppose the very identity the memory was supposed to establish. Each objection chips at the confidence with which we say that the rememberer and the remembered are simply one.

The repairs proposed are instructive. Perhaps what matters is not direct memory but overlapping chains of it, each day linked to the next even when the ends do not touch. Perhaps, more radically, the self is less a thing we possess than a story we keep composing, a narrative that selects and orders experiences into something that feels continuous. I am drawn to this last suggestion, with a caution: if the self is a story, it is one whose author is bound by facts she did not invent, and a life is not made coherent merely by being told well. Identity may be narrated, but it is not therefore fiction. We are, perhaps, both the tale and its teller, and neither role makes full sense without the other.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: Time Made Story
passageSets.passage:
Raw time is only one thing after another, an undifferentiated succession of moments with no seams and no shape. Yet no one experiences a life that way. We instinctively carve the stream into episodes, assign them beginnings and endings, and bind them with the connective tissue of because and therefore. We do not merely live through events; we narrate them, turning succession into story almost as automatically as we turn sound into speech. The impulse is so deep that we scarcely recognize it as an impulse at all.

The reason is not hard to find. A sequence tells you only what came after what; a story tells you what led to what, and it is meaning we are after, not mere order. To emplot a series of happenings is to claim that they hang together, that the earlier explains the later and the whole arrives somewhere. Narrative is the form in which time becomes intelligible rather than simply endured, the device by which a scatter of moments is gathered into an experience that can be understood, remembered, and told again. Order asks only when; story asks why, and it is the why that we can live by.

So much of what we value rides on this gathering. A self is in large part the story a person tells about who she has been and is becoming; memory preserves not raw footage but edited scenes; and moral understanding depends on our grasping how choices ramify into consequences over time. Take away the narrative frame and these would not shrink but shatter. To understand a human being at all is to follow a story, which is why the arts of narrative feel less like entertainment than like instruments of comprehension. Strip away the arc and a life becomes a heap of instants that no one could inhabit as a life.

And yet the very power of the form is also its danger. Lives do not really arrive with tidy beginnings, climaxes, and resolutions; those are shapes we impose, and the imposition can falsify as easily as it clarifies. We are tempted to see destiny in what was accident, and to trim the recalcitrant facts that spoil a satisfying arc. The mature use of narrative therefore requires a divided mind: to recognize that we cannot understand our lives without storytelling, and to remember, at the same time, that the story is a made thing and that the life always exceeds it. We are the creatures that cannot help emplotting time, and wisdom lies not in stopping but in never mistaking the plot for the whole.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: Fact and Vision
passageSets.passage:
Literary history is often told as a duel between two temperaments. On one side stands realism, pledged to show life as it actually is: ordinary people, unremarkable rooms, the slow grinding of circumstance, the refusal of tidy endings. On the other stands romanticism, impatient with the merely actual, reaching after intensity, aspiration, and the heightened truths of feeling that daily life obscures. The realist accuses the romantic of pleasant lies; the romantic accuses the realist of a dreary literalism that mistakes the surface of things for their meaning.

Realism draws its authority from honesty. It promises not to flatter, to attend to the overlooked and the inconvenient, to grant the texture of a real life its due weight. Against sentimental fantasy it offers the bracing dignity of the true, and it flatters the reader with the sense that here, at last, is the world without cosmetics. Much of the moral force of the novel has come from this refusal to look away from what is ordinary, difficult, or unjust. Its authority is the authority of the witness who will not soften the testimony.

Romanticism answers that a photograph of the surface is not the truth but only its skin. Feeling, longing, and the imagination of what might be are as real as any tenement or ledger, and a literature that excludes them in the name of accuracy has purchased its honesty by amputating half of experience. The romantic insists that to render only what is, and never what is desired, feared, or dreamed, is itself a distortion, and a peculiarly bloodless one. To it, a literature of mere inventory is a betrayal of everything that makes life worth recording.

Stated as opposites, the two positions caricature each other, and the caricature dissolves on inspection. No realism is a mere transcript; the realist selects, arranges, and shades, and every such choice is an act of the shaping imagination the romantic prizes. No romanticism floats entirely free; its heightened feelings must be anchored in situations we recognize, or they move no one. The interesting writers have always drawn on both, disciplining vision with fact and quickening fact with vision. The duel, in the end, is less a war between camps than a tension within any serious act of writing, and perhaps within perception itself. The greatest works refuse the choice, seeing clearly and feeling deeply in the same breath.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage's central thesis is that realism and romanticism are:

choices.label: A

choices.label: B
choices.text: complementary tendencies that combine in any serious writing

choices.label: C
choices.text: outdated categories with no modern relevance

choices.label: D
choices.text: identical in their aims and methods

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author concludes that serious writers draw on both, 'disciplining vision with fact and quickening fact with vision.' A is wrong because the author dismantles the strict opposition. C is wrong because no claim of obsolescence is made. D is wrong because the two differ in emphasis; they are complementary, not identical.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's tone in treating the two schools is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: partisan toward realism

choices.label: B
choices.text: dismissive of both

choices.label: C
choices.text: anxious

choices.label: D
choices.text: evenhanded and reconciling

questions.explanation: D is correct: the author gives each school its strongest case and then unites them, an evenhanded and reconciling posture. A is wrong because the author favors neither side. B is wrong because both schools are treated with respect. C is wrong because the tone is measured, not anxious.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The second and third paragraphs function together to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: prove that realism is superior to romanticism

choices.label: B
choices.text: define the technical vocabulary of literary criticism

choices.label: C
choices.text: present the strongest case for each school before the author reconciles them

choices.label: D
choices.text: trace the chronological development of the novel

questions.explanation: C is correct: one paragraph makes realism's case and the next makes romanticism's, setting up the reconciliation that follows. A is wrong because no superiority is argued. B is wrong because the paragraphs argue rather than define terms. D is wrong because they present competing cases, not a historical timeline.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: A novelist who documents slum poverty in precise detail but arranges those details to stir moral outrage would, in the author's view, demonstrate that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: realism itself relies on the shaping imagination the romantic prizes

choices.label: B
choices.text: romanticism cannot depict social problems

choices.label: C
choices.text: realism and romanticism never appear together

choices.label: D
choices.text: accurate description requires no choices

questions.explanation: A is correct: the author says 'no realism is a mere transcript,' since the realist 'selects, arranges, and shades,' an act of shaping imagination. B is wrong because the scenario concerns realism's methods, not romanticism's limits. C is wrong because the example shows the two combining, contradicting the claim of separation. D is wrong because the author insists such selection always involves choices.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which claim, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's reconciliation of the two schools?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Great novels routinely blend accurate detail with heightened feeling

choices.label: B
choices.text: Realist writers make deliberate choices about what to include

choices.label: C
choices.text: Romantic works are set in recognizable situations

choices.label: D
choices.text: The most admired works of each school share no features and succeed only by strictly excluding the other's methods

questions.explanation: D is correct: the reconciliation depends on the two tendencies blending, so evidence that great works strictly exclude each other would undermine it. A is wrong because blending supports the author's thesis. B is wrong because deliberate selection is the shaping imagination the author cites in favor of blending. C is wrong because recognizable settings support the claim that romanticism is anchored in fact.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: In the third paragraph, saying a photograph shows 'only its skin' suggests that pure surface accuracy:

choices.label: A
choices.text: captures the deepest truth of a subject

choices.label: B
choices.text: misses the inner or deeper reality beneath appearances

choices.label: C
choices.text: is technically impossible to achieve

choices.label: D
choices.text: is the highest aim of romanticism

questions.explanation: B is correct: 'skin' names mere surface, so the romantic charge is that accuracy alone misses the truth beneath. A is wrong because it reverses the intended meaning. C is wrong because feasibility is not the issue raised. D is wrong because the phrase voices romanticism's critique of realism, not romanticism's own aim.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

passageSets.title: On Trial: The Humanities
passageSets.passage:
In an age that asks every pursuit to prove its usefulness, the study of literature, history, and philosophy is repeatedly summoned to the dock. What, the prosecution demands, is it good for? The question is usually posed in economic terms, and answered, by anxious defenders, in the same terms: graduates in the humanities, they hasten to show, earn respectable salaries and acquire skills that employers prize. This defense is not false, but it is a trap, for it concedes the very premise it ought to challenge, that the worth of a study is settled by its cash value. The demand sounds reasonable, and that is exactly what makes it so difficult to answer well.

There is something to the utilitarian case, and pretending otherwise only discredits the humanities further. A person must earn a living, and a course of study that ignored the future entirely would be a dereliction. Reading closely, writing clearly, and reasoning about evidence are genuinely marketable, and it is no betrayal to say so. The error is not in noting these benefits but in resting the whole defense upon them, as though nothing further could be said once the ledger balanced.

For the deeper value of the humanities is precisely what the ledger cannot record. To read widely is to inhabit, for a time, minds and eras not one's own, and to return less imprisoned by the accident of one's birth. To weigh an argument, interpret a difficult text, or judge a historical actor is to practice the discrimination on which a free and reflective life depends. These studies do not merely equip us to make a living; they help us decide what a living is for. That is not a soft supplement to practical education but its missing center. Such capacities are not luxuries added to a life but the very faculties by which a life is steered.

Honesty, however, forbids overstatement. The humanities do not automatically produce virtue; the learned can be cruel, and a shelf of great books guarantees nothing. What these studies offer is an opportunity, not a result: the materials and the practice from which judgment, sympathy, and self-knowledge may grow in a person willing to be changed. Defended in those terms, without apology and without inflation, the humanities need not borrow their dignity from the marketplace. They answer a different question than the one the prosecution keeps asking, and it is the more important question. To ask only what the humanities pay is to have forgotten, already, what they are for.
passageSets.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The passage's principal argument is that the humanities:

choices.label: A
choices.text: should be defended solely by their economic payoff

choices.label: B
choices.text: have no practical value whatsoever

choices.label: C
choices.text: possess a value that economic measures cannot capture and should be defended on those deeper grounds

choices.label: D
choices.text: automatically make students more virtuous

questions.explanation: C is correct: the author holds that 'the deeper value of the humanities is precisely what the ledger cannot record' and urges defending them there. A is wrong because the author calls the purely economic defense a 'trap.' B is wrong because the author grants that humanities skills are genuinely marketable. D is wrong because the final paragraph denies any automatic production of virtue.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the purely economic defense of the humanities is:

choices.label: A
choices.text: sympathetic to its partial truth but critical of its adequacy

choices.label: B
choices.text: entirely dismissive

choices.label: C
choices.text: wholeheartedly approving

choices.label: D
choices.text: indifferent

questions.explanation: A is correct: the author concedes 'there is something to the utilitarian case' yet insists that resting on it is a trap, both sympathy and criticism. B is wrong because the author grants the case a partial truth. C is wrong because the author warns the defense concedes too much. D is wrong because the author is deeply invested in the question.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: The concession in the second paragraph that reading and reasoning are 'marketable' serves to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: abandon the author's larger argument

choices.label: B
choices.text: prove the prosecution entirely correct

choices.label: C
choices.text: show that the humanities have no other value

choices.label: D
choices.text: strengthen the author's credibility before advancing a deeper claim

questions.explanation: D is correct: granting the partial truth guards against overstatement and prepares the turn to the humanities' deeper value. A is wrong because the author continues, rather than abandons, the argument. B is wrong because the author still challenges the economic premise. C is wrong because the next paragraph asserts a value beyond the marketable.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: It can be inferred that the author regards a defense of the humanities based only on salaries as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: the strongest possible argument

choices.label: B
choices.text: self-defeating, because it accepts the very standard it should contest

choices.label: C
choices.text: irrelevant to how the humanities are actually valued

choices.label: D
choices.text: dishonest and factually false

questions.explanation: B is correct: the author says such a defense 'concedes the very premise it ought to challenge,' making it self-defeating. A is wrong because the author treats it as a trap, not the strongest case. C is wrong because the author engages it as consequential, not irrelevant. D is wrong because the author explicitly calls the economic claim 'not false.'
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which claim would most strengthen the author's view that the humanities offer an opportunity rather than a guaranteed result?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reading great literature improves empathy only for readers who engage reflectively, not for everyone assigned it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Humanities graduates always earn more than other graduates

choices.label: C
choices.text: Every person who studies philosophy becomes morally better

choices.label: D
choices.text: Employers rank humanities skills above technical skills

questions.explanation: A is correct: it shows the benefit depends on the willing, reflective reader, matching 'an opportunity, not a result.' B is wrong because it concerns economic payoff, the defense the author resists. C is wrong because it contradicts the author's denial of automatic virtue. D is wrong because it again rests on marketable value rather than the deeper claim.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: In context, the courtroom images ('summoned to the dock', 'the prosecution') characterize the humanities as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: guilty of wasting public money

choices.label: B
choices.text: the judges of other disciplines

choices.label: C
choices.text: forced to justify their existence against skeptical demands

choices.label: D
choices.text: universally admired institutions

questions.explanation: C is correct: the courtroom metaphor casts the humanities as a defendant made to prove their worth to skeptics. A is wrong because the author does not concede guilt. B is wrong because the humanities are on trial, not presiding. D is wrong because the imagery signals suspicion, the opposite of universal admiration.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content
